intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users of health dashboards prefer seeing data points across time (timeline view) rather than summarized metrics in cards
facade.label: Timeline with numbers
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should prioritize chronological progression and raw data visibility over aggregated summaries
facade.rejectImplies: The dashboard should emphasize high-level insights, trends, and consolidated metrics over granular historical tracking

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards benefit from gentle, approachable visual softness rather than clinical sharpness
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: Explore warmer, more human-feeling interfaces with curves, soft shadows, and forgiving aesthetics
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer cleaner, more structured look with defined edges and higher contrast — lean into minimal geometry

evidence.hypothesis: Users of health dashboards prefer seeing data points across time (timeline view) rather than summarized metrics in cards
evidence.implication: The dashboard should prioritize chronological progression and raw data visibility over aggregated summaries

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, animated state changes over instant updates when logging health data, as it creates continuity and reduces cognitive load
facade.axisTargeted: Fluid animation vs instant state change
facade.label: Smooth slide transitions
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into transitions for data entry, metric updates, and view switches—make motion feel like liquid rather than snappy
facade.rejectImplies: Users want speed and directness over visual smoothing—use instant updates and minimal animation for a no-nonsense dashboard feel

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left navigation column paired with card-based content layout will feel organized and reduce cognitive load for users tracking multiple experiments
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation persistence vs. tab-based switching; card-based chunking vs. list-based density
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers stable, always-visible navigation anchors and discrete content modules for experiment grouping
facade.rejectImplies: User may favor flat information hierarchies, tabbed interfaces, or denser list views that maximize content space

facade.hypothesis: The mood lands warmer and more organic when data visualization uses rounded corners, gentle color gradients, and loose illustration styles rather than sharp gridlines and flat geometric forms.
facade.label: Soft graphs and charts
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers humanized data representation—curves over rectangles, illustration over pure information design, something that feels approachable rather than clinical.
facade.rejectImplies: User gravitates toward clarity-first design—crisp grids, high contrast, precision. Data should feel authoritative and efficient, not soft or illustrated.

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, conversational voice with simple language will feel more motivating for daily health tracking than clinical terminology.
facade.label: Casual check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should use warm, encouraging language, plain English, and a supportive tone that treats tracking as a personal conversation rather than a medical report.
facade.rejectImplies: The dashboard should adopt a clinical, precise, data-forward voice with technical health terms and neutral tone to maintain credibility and focus on measurable outcomes.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer dark interfaces for nighttime data review and reduced eye strain during frequent dashboard checks
facade.label: Dark mode
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should offer a dark theme as default or prominent option, prioritizing reduced cognitive load and eye comfort for repeated monitoring sessions
facade.rejectImplies: The dashboard should maintain a bright, high-contrast interface to ensure clarity and clinical readability of health metrics, with dark mode as unnecessary personalization
